subj-group.subject: Traditional Medicine, Complementary and Integrative Medicine

title-group.article-title: Utilizing the DMN and DAN to Study the Effects of Acupuncture on Patients with Cognitive Impairment in Long COVID: A Pragmatic Randomized Controlled Trial Protocol

trans-title-group.trans-title: Nutzung des DMN und DAN zur Untersuchung der Effekte von Akupunktur bei Patientinnen und Patienten mit kognitiven Beeinträchtigungen nach Long COVID: Protokoll einer pragmatischen, randomisierten kontrollierten Studie
Utilisation du DMN et du DAN pour étudier les effets de l’acupuncture chez les patients présentant des troubles cognitifs liés au long COVID : protocole d’un essai contrôlé randomisé pragmatique

title-group.alt-title: Acupuncture Therapy for Cognitive Impairment in Long COVID

contrib-group.aff: aFirst Affiliated Hospital of Bengbu Medical College, Bengbu, China
bDepartment of Rehabilitation, The People’s Hospital of Baoan Shenzhen, Bengbu Medical College, Shenzhen, China
cGuangzhou University of Chinese Medicine, Guangzhou, China

author-notes.corresp: Correspondence to: Lei Xu, xuleibyfy@163.com or Yingqi Lu, lyq01232023@163.com or Shangjie Chen, csjmesz@sina.com

fn.p: Jing Chen and Yanyu Wu contributed equally to this work and should be regarded as equivalent first authors.

Trial registration: Chinese Clinical Trial Registry (http://www.chictr.org.cn), Registration number: ChiCTR2400092961, date of registration: 2024-11-26. For the SPIRIT-Outcomes 2022 Checklist, see online supplementary material at https://doi.org/10.1159/000549822.

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by S. Karger AG, Basel

license.license-p: This article is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International License (CC BY-NC) (https://karger.com/Services/OpenAccessLicense). Usage and distribution for commercial purposes requires written permission.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Cognitive impairment is one of the long COVID symptoms that many people experience after Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19). Many individuals report a decline in cognitive functions, such as reduced memory and brain fog. These symptoms not only directly affect the cognitive functions of the brain but also hinder daily living activities, thereby reducing the quality of life. Moreover, these symptoms are significant risk factors for long-term cognitive decline in the elderly and can have both short-term and long-term effects on brain function. Clinically, acupuncture is widely used to improve cognitive impairment in the elderly. Elucidating the brain network mechanisms underlying acupuncture therapy for long COVID-related cognitive impairment represents an urgently needed research focus. In this study, we employed acupuncture as an intervention to mitigate cognitive decline in long COVID patients and investigate the potential mechanisms by which acupuncture alleviates cognitive impairment.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: In this randomized controlled trial, 60 eligible participants are planned to be recruited and randomly assigned in a 1:1 ratio to the acupuncture group and the health education group, which will then receive acupuncture treatment and health education. The acupuncture group will participate in treatment three times per week for a total of 8 weeks. The health education group will receive health education once per week for a total of 8 weeks. The primary assessment index was the Montreal Cognitive Assessment Scale (MoCA), and the secondary assessment indexes included Clinical Dementia Rating (CDR), Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE), Activity of Daily Living Scale (ADL), Auditory Verbal Learning Test-Huashan Version (AVLT-H), and resting-state functional magnetic resonance imaging data. These assessment indicators were all tested in 1 week each before and after the intervention was implemented.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This trial aims to investigate the therapeutic effects of acupuncture on cognitive impairment in patients with long COVID and to further explore the imaging mechanisms by which acupuncture alleviates cognitive dysfunction in these patients.

sec.title: Hintergrund
sec.p: Kognitive Beeinträchtigungen zählen zu den häufigen Symptomen von Long COVID, die viele Menschen nach einer Infektion mit dem Coronavirus SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19) erleben. Zahlreiche Betroffene berichten „über nachlassende Gedächtnisleistungen und Konzentrationsstörungen“ („brain fog“). Diese Symptome beeinträchtigen nicht nur direkt die kognitiven Funktionen des Gehirns, sondern erschweren auch alltägliche Aktivitäten und führen zu einer verminderten Lebensqualität. Darüber hinaus stellen sie bedeutende Risikofaktoren für einen langfristigen kognitiven Abbau im höheren Lebensalter dar und können sowohl kurz-als auch langfristige Auswirkungen auf die Hirnfunktion haben. In der klinischen Praxis wird Akupunktur häufig zur Verbesserung kognitiver Beeinträchtigungen bei älteren Menschen eingesetzt. Die Aufklärung der zugrunde liegenden Mechanismen auf Ebene der Hirnnetzwerke bei der Akupunkturtherapie Long COVID-assoziierter kognitiver Störungen stellt ein dringendes Forschungsanliegen dar. In der vorliegenden Studie wird Akupunktur als Intervention eingesetzt, um den kognitiven Abbau bei Long COVID-Patientinnen und -Patienten zu mildern und die potenziellen Mechanismen der Akupunktur zur Linderung kognitiver Beeinträchtigungen zu untersuchen.

sec.title: Methoden
sec.p: In dieser randomisierten kontrollierten Studie sollen 60 geeignete Teilnehmende rekrutiert und im Verhältnis 1:1 randomisiert der Akupunkturgruppe bzw. der Gesundheitsbildungsgruppe zugeteilt werden. Die Akupunkturgruppe erhält dreimal wöchentlich über insgesamt acht Wochen eine Akupunkturbehandlung. Die Kontrollgruppe erhält einmal wöchentlich über acht Wochen eine Gesundheitsbildung. Der primäre Endpunkt ist die Montreal Cognitive Assessment Scale (MoCA). Sekundäre Endpunkte umfassen die Clinical Dementia Rating (CDR), den Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE), die Skala für Aktivitäten des täglichen Lebens (ADL), den Auditory Verbal Learning Test – Huashan Version (AVLT-H) sowie Daten der funktionellen Magnetresonanztomographie im Ruhezustand (rs-fMRT). Alle Messungen erfolgen jeweils eine Woche vor und nach der Intervention.

sec.title: Diskussion
sec.p: Diese Studie zielt darauf ab, die therapeutischen Effekte der Akupunktur auf kognitive Beeinträchtigungen bei Patientinnen und Patienten mit Long COVID zu untersuchen und die bildgebenden Mechanismen zu erforschen, durch die Akupunktur kognitive Dysfunktionen in dieser Patientengruppe lindert.

sec.title: Contexte
sec.p: Le trouble cognitif constitue l’un des symptômes du long COVID que de nombreuses personnes présentent après une infection par le SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19). De nombreux individus rapportent une diminution des fonctions cognitives, telle qu’une mémoire réduite ou un « brouillard cérébral ». Ces symptômes affectent non seulement directement les fonctions cognitives du cerveau, mais entravent également les activités de la vie quotidienne, réduisant ainsi la qualité de vie. Par ailleurs, ces manifestations représentent des facteurs de risque majeurs pour un déclin cognitif à long terme chez les personnes âgées et peuvent avoir des répercussions tant à court qu’à long terme sur la fonction cérébrale. En pratique clinique, l’acupuncture est largement utilisée pour améliorer les troubles cognitifs chez les sujets âgés. L’élucidation des mécanismes des réseaux cérébraux sous-jacents à la thérapie par acupuncture dans le cadre des troubles cognitifs associés au long COVID représente un axe de recherche prioritaire. Dans cette étude, nous avons utilisé l’acupuncture comme intervention afin d’atténuer le déclin cognitif chez les patients atteints de long COVID et d’explorer les mécanismes potentiels par lesquels l’acupuncture pourrait soulager les troubles cognitifs.

sec.title: Méthodes
sec.p: Dans cet essai contrôlé randomisé, il est prévu de recruter soixante participants éligibles, répartis de façon aléatoire selon un ratio 1 : 1 entre un groupe acupuncture et un groupe éducation à la santé, lesquels recevront respectivement un traitement par acupuncture ou une éducation à la santé. Le groupe acupuncture bénéficiera de trois séances hebdomadaires pendant huit semaines. Le groupe éducation à la santé recevra une séance hebdomadaire sur la même période. Le critère d’évaluation principal est l’échelle d’évaluation cognitive de Montréal (MoCA) ; les critères secondaires comprennent la Clinical Dementia Rating (CDR), le Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE), l’échelle des activités de la vie quotidienne (ADL), l’Auditory Verbal Learning Test – version Huashan (AVLT-H) ainsi que les données d’imagerie par résonance magnétique fonctionnelle au repos (rs-IRMf). Tous ces critères ont été évalués une semaine avant et une semaine après l’intervention.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Cet essai vise à évaluer les effets thérapeutiques de l’acupuncture sur les troubles cognitifs chez les patients atteints de long COVID et à approfondir l’exploration des mécanismes d’imagerie par lesquels l’acupuncture pourrait atténuer la dysfonction cognitive dans cette population.

funding-group.funding-statement: Funding sources were as follows: National Natural Science Foundation of China (81973922); Shenzhen Bao'an District Medical Association (BAYXH2023011); Shenzhen Bao’an District Traditional Chinese Medicine Development Foundation (2022KJCX-ZJZL-14); and Shenzhen Sanming Project (2022).

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The infection caused by severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) has led to a sharp increase in cases of COVID-19, placing a heavy burden on global healthcare systems [1, 2]. Although many infected individuals have recovered, a significant number of people continue to experience symptoms and complications after the acute phase [3, 4]. Many patients are currently suffering or have previously suffered from long COVID syndrome [5, 6]. This public health crisis has severely threatened people’s mental health and quality of life and has imposed an inexpressible burden on society and the nation.

Within the 12 months following discharge from COVID-19, approximately 3.3% of COVID-19 survivors were diagnosed with dementia, while 9.1% of patients experienced mild cognitive impairment (MCI). Particularly in severe cases, the incidence rates of dementia and MCI were as high as 15.00% and 26.15%, respectively [7]. Three months following the onset of possible or confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection, with symptoms persisting for at least 2 months, patients exhibited overall cognitive impairment and episodic memory deficits, particularly in attention, working memory, and processing speed [6, 8, 9]. Additionally, neuroimaging of the brain revealed lower fractional anisotropy and higher radial diffusivity in white matter regions [10, 11]. Following severe SARS-CoV-2 infection, patients may develop chronic systemic inflammation, which can exacerbate neurodegeneration [12]. The resulting cognitive impairment is comparable to the natural cognitive decline that occurs between the ages of 50 and 70, and this impact can still be detected 6 months later [13].

Whether individuals are symptomatic or asymptomatic, COVID-19 may have both short-term and long-term effects on the brain function of patients [14]. In imaging studies, it has been observed that, compared to healthy individuals, patients with long COVID exhibit reduced connectivity between the left and right par hippocampal gyri, as well as decreased functional brain connections between the bilateral orbitofrontal regions and the cerebellum [11]. In addition, there is a more pronounced reduction in gray matter thickness of the orbitofrontal cortex and the par hippocampal gyrus, a decrease in overall brain volume, and more significant changes in tissue damage markers in areas related to the function of the primary olfactory cortex [15]. There is further evidence to suggest that the impact of SARS-CoV-2 on the central nervous system may be a key factor in the risk of cognitive decline in later life, Alzheimer’s disease, and other forms of dementia [16–18].

Acupuncture, as a common and green intervention method, has extensive regulatory effects on the human body. Based on bioinformatics research, it has been found that acupuncture can treat COVID-19 by inhibiting inflammatory stress, enhancing immunity, and regulating nervous system function through the activation of neuroactive ligand-receptor interactions, calcium signaling pathways, and cancer pathways, among others. This research has, for the first time, elucidated the multiple synergistic mechanisms of acupuncture in treating COVID-19, indicating that acupuncture may play a positive role in the treatment of COVID-19 [19]. In addition, a systematic review that included several randomized controlled trials also showed that acupuncture can improve symptoms such as fatigue, cognitive impairment, depression, and anxiety and may be applicable to patients with long COVID [20]. In order to further investigate the efficacy of acupuncture in treating cognitive impairment in long COVID patients, we proposed this randomized controlled trial. In this study, we provided acupuncture treatment for patients with MCI due to long COVID, aiming to alleviate cognitive impairment in these patients. At the same time, we utilized functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) technology to explore the potential mechanisms of acupuncture in treating MCI in long COVID patients from the perspective of brain networks.

sec.title: Study Design
sec.p: This study employed a randomized controlled trial, including allocation concealment and assessor blinding, and recruited 60 eligible patients who met the diagnostic criteria for MCI due to long COVID from the local area, in accordance with the inclusion criteria. These patients were randomly allocated to two groups using the random number table method: acupuncture group and health education group, with 30 participants each. Sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed envelopes were created to ensure allocation concealment. During the trial, the experimenters, recorders, and assessors were independent of each other, and researchers responsible for experimental operations, data collection, and data statistics were not allowed to exchange their respective work content. The control group only received routine health education and did not undergo any other form of intervention. The acupuncture group received acupuncture treatment in addition to routine health education. At the beginning and end of the study, all participants who met the inclusion criteria underwent a comprehensive cognitive assessment. For a comprehensive understanding of the study design and the timetable for outcome assessment, please refer to Figures 1 and 2.

caption.p: Schedule of enrollment, assessments, and interventions.

sec.title: Inclusion Criteria
sec.p: Patients with MCI due to long COVID should meet the following criteria: (1) Older adults with MCI following SARS-CoV-2 infection at 3 months; (2) aged between 60 and 75 years; (3) educational level above middle school; (4) health checkup in a tertiary hospital or above within the past 6 months proving good health; (5) body mass index within the range of 20–24; (6) right handed; (7) signed an informed consent form.

sec.title: Long COVID Diagnostic Criteria
sec.p: Symptoms of long COVID typically occur in individuals with a history of SARS-CoV-2 infection, whether suspected or confirmed. These symptoms generally manifest 3 months after the onset of COVID-19 and persist for at least 2 months, with no other diagnosis able to explain them. Common symptoms include fatigue, shortness of breath, and cognitive impairment, which often interfere with daily activities. These symptoms may either newly emerge during the initial recovery period following acute COVID-19 or persist from the initial illness. Additionally, the symptoms may fluctuate over time or relapse [21].

The diagnostic criteria for MCI refer to the criteria proposed by the International Working Group in 2003, as recommended in the 2018 Chinese Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Treatment of Dementia and Cognitive Impairment [22]: (1) Memory impairment is the chief complaint, and it is confirmed by an informant. (2) There is an objective decline in memory that is not consistent with age. (3) There is impairment in one or more cognitive domains (memory, language, visuospatial abilities, or executive function). (4) Daily living abilities are essentially normal. (5) The degree of cognitive impairment is not sufficient to diagnose dementia.

sec.title: Exclusion Criteria
sec.p: Exclusion criteria were as follows: (1) Individuals with metal residues in the body or tattoos, as well as those with other conditions that make them unsuitable for magnetic resonance imaging; (2) individuals who have a fear of fMRI, or who are unable to complete the fMRI scan for other reasons; (3) individuals in whom brain examinations reveal significant or suspicious lesions; (4) individuals with a history of heavy alcohol use or drug abuse; (5) individuals with a history of mental illness or congenital mental retardation, including those who have taken or are currently taking psychotropic medications such as anti-anxiety or antidepressant drugs within the past 3 months, patients undergoing psychotherapy, and individuals using medications and treatments that may significantly affect cognition; (6) individuals suffering from severe cardiac, hepatic, or renal diseases, or other neurological disorders that may cause cognitive impairment, such as brain tumors, encephalitis, epilepsy, Parkinson’s disease, etc.

sec.title: Termination Criteria
sec.p: Treatment criteria were as follows: (1) Participants who withdraw from the study for any reason midway; (2) participants with poor compliance who are unable to complete the treatment; (3) participants who are lost to follow-up; (4) participants with incomplete data. Any participant who meets any of the above criteria will be terminated from the study or excluded from this research.

sec.title: Rationale for the Pragmatic Design
sec.p: Our previous investigations and numerous similar studies [23–25] have consistently revealed that, within the realm of neurocognitive research, sham acupuncture groups – specifically those incorporating appropriate “penetrative” or “non-meridian, non-acupoint” designs – fail to demonstrate any clinically meaningful or sustained therapeutic effects. These effects are assessed through objective cognitive measures like ADAS-Cog and MoCA scores, along with neuroimaging connectivity features, as well as patient-reported improvements in cognitive function, with no significant impacts observed beyond baseline levels. Such evidence strongly implies that the placebo effect of sham acupuncture may be weak and unstable when targeting complex neurocognitive enhancements, differing fundamentally from true acupuncture in its mechanisms, which involve specific neurological pathway integration. In actual clinical settings, patients grappling with long COVID-related cognitive impairment typically face a core choice: opting for acupuncture treatment or foregoing it altogether, rather than selecting between genuine and sham approaches. Factors such as time constraints, preconceived notions about acupuncture, procedural apprehensions, and transportation accessibility directly shape their willingness to engage with the therapy. We fully recognize the value of placebo controls in randomized controlled trials, particularly their importance for verifying specific therapeutic mechanisms. However, in this study where the primary objective is to evaluate the clinical utility of acupuncture in managing Long COVID-related cognitive impairment, we implemented a pragmatic randomized controlled trial design comparing an acupuncture group versus a health education group.

sec.title: Recruitment
sec.p: To attract eligible participants, researchers employed a variety of strategies, including organizing free clinics, posting recruitment information on WeChat Moments, putting up promotional posters, distributing flyers in the community, and conducting on-site presentations. These recruitment activities were primarily conducted at The Second Affiliated Hospital of Shenzhen University and its affiliated Xin'an Street Community Health Service Center. During the recruitment process, staff members who had undergone professional training would provide potential participants with a detailed introduction to the specific content of the study. They would also ensure that these individuals signed an informed consent form before agreeing to participate, confirming that they had fully understood the relevant information about the study and were participating voluntarily.

sec.title: Randomization, Allocation Concealment, and Blinding
sec.p: In this study, we generated a random allocation sequence using the random number table method to assign participants to different groups. The researcher managing this randomization sequence did not participate in the recruitment, assessment, or intervention processes and was independently responsible for the entire sequence management, informing participants of their group assignments. Given the nature of acupuncture treatment, it was not feasible to achieve complete double blinding for all participants and the medical staff administering acupuncture during the treatment phase of this study; however, the assessors and statisticians responsible for data analysis remained unaware of the participants’ group assignments. After the statistical analysis was completed, the treatment group information of the participants was revealed for analysis and summary.

sec.title: Sample Size
sec.p: The MoCA score served as the primary outcome measure for evaluating participants’ cognitive function. Based on existing literature, the MoCA is reported to have a large Cohen’s effect size [26]. Using G*Power 3.1 software, a sample size calculation was performed with a two-sided α of 0.05 and a power of 0.8, yielding a required sample size of 52 participants. Accounting for a 10–20% attrition rate, the total sample size for this study was set at 60 participants. Furthermore, according to a study by Desmond et al. [27], for fMRI experiments with multiple comparisons corrected at a two-tailed α of 0.05, a minimum of 24 subjects is required to achieve an 80% activation detection rate at the individual level. After considering a potential dropout rate, the adjusted sample size was 29 participants. To ensure higher validity and robustness of the findings, the final sample size selected for this study was 60 participants.

sec.title: Acupuncture Group
sec.p: Acupuncture treatment was conducted in the acupuncture clinic of The Second Affiliated Hospital of Shenzhen University, once on each Monday, Wednesday, and Friday, that is, every other day, for a total of three sessions per week. The entire treatment process consisted of four courses, each lasting 2 weeks. During the treatment process, the number of treatments for participants is recorded by research assistants, who also promptly remind the participants to attend the sessions. The treatment was performed by a licensed traditional Chinese medicine practitioner with over 15 years of acupuncture experience, using disposable Hua Tuo brand acupuncture needles (specifications of 0.25 × 25 mm or 0.30 × 40 mm). Do not engage in other treatments. The selected acupoints included EX-HN1, EX-HN3, bilateral PC6, bilateral ST40, bilateral SP6, bilateral KI3, and bilateral LR3. The specific locations of the acupuncture points can be found in Figure 3. Participants were positioned supine, and the skin was routinely disinfected before needling. After needle insertion, the corresponding acupuncture technique is applied based on ensuring that the participant experiences the sensation of deqi. The specific procedures are as follows: for the EX-HN3 points, insert the needle horizontally 0.5 transverse finger width toward the GV20 point along the scalp, using the twisting and rotating reducing method. The procedure for the EX-HN3 point is as follows: first, insert the needle vertically and shallowly into the subcutaneous layer, using the sparrow pecking reducing method until the eyes become moist. Then, pinch the skin and insert the needle horizontally 0.5 transverse finger width toward the tip of the nose. The PC6 point is needled vertically to a depth of 0.5–1.0 transverse finger width, using the twisting and rotating reducing method. The ST40 point is needled vertically to a depth of 1–1.5 transverse finger widths, employing the combined twisting, lifting, and inserting reducing method. For the SP6 point, insert the needle along the posterior edge of the medial side of the tibia at a 45°angle to the skin and needle obliquely backward to a depth of 1.0–1.5 transverse finger widths, using the lifting and inserting tonifying method. The KI3 point is needled vertically to a depth of 0.5 transverse finger width, using the twisting and rotating tonifying method. The LR3 point is needled vertically to a depth of 0.5 transverse finger width, using the twisting and rotating reducing method. During each treatment session, each acupoint is needed for 1 min, followed by a 30-min retention period. The needles are removed after the retention period. During the retention period, the needles are manipulated every 10 min.

caption.p: Green circular markers indicate the acupoint locations for the acupuncture group: EX-HN1, EX-HN3, PC6, ST40, SP6, KI3, and LR3.

sec.title: Health Education Group
sec.p: The control group received only conventional health education without any additional special interventions. The education was conducted once a week for a total of 8 weeks. The content of the health education covered the symptoms that COVID-19 may cause, how to maintain a healthy lifestyle in the face of subhealth conditions, and the importance of early prevention of Alzheimer’s disease.

sec.title: Assessment of Results
sec.p: The variables considered in this trial include the participants’ basic information, primary outcomes, and secondary outcomes. Baseline characteristics will be assessed within 1 week prior to randomization. Primary and secondary outcomes will be evaluated within 1 week before and after the participants receive acupuncture treatment. All these assessments will be conducted by multiple members of the research team with extensive experience, reducing the subjectivity of the assessment indicators. These assessors will remain blinded to the group assignments of all participants to ensure the integrity and objectivity of the study.

sec.title: Outcome Indicators
sec.p: This study utilized the Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) as the primary outcome measurement tool. Designed for rapid assessment within 10 min, this scale provides a standardized evaluation of overall cognitive function in MCI patients, covering seven domains: visuospatial/executive function, naming, verbal memory, attention, abstract thinking, delayed recall, and orientation. Scoring from 0 to 30 (with one point added for individuals with ≤12 years of education), the MoCA yields lower scores correlating with more severe cognitive impairment [28]. Secondary outcomes employed a multidimensional assessment system. Quantifying dementia severity, the Clinical Dementia Rating (CDR) assesses six domains spanning memory, orientation, judgment and problem-solving, community affairs, home and hobbies, and personal care, producing a total score (0–3) corresponding to the spectrum from normal to severe dementia [29]. For initial screening, the Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) screens for cognitive deficits using a 0–30 scale (scores <27 suggestive of impairment), offering a rapid and effective diagnostic reference [30]. Evaluating independence and rehabilitation needs, the Activities of Daily Living Scale (ADL) [31] measures both basic (BADL) and instrumental (IADL) activities, with higher total scores reflecting greater functional impairment. Focusing on sensitive indicators like long-delay recall (20 min) and recognition, the Huashan version Auditory Verbal Learning Test (AVLT-H) precisely assesses episodic memory function through the memory quotient (MQ), thereby providing specific evidence for identifying MCI [32].

sec.title: Resting-State fMRI Data
sec.p: After head fixation, resting-state functional brain imaging was initiated using a gradient echo-planar imaging sequence for axial anatomical imaging. The data acquisition slices were parallel to the line connecting the anterior and posterior commissures, ensuring full coverage of the entire brain region. All participants were instructed to remain relaxed and motionless during the scanning process. The specific scanning parameters were set as follows: repetition time = 2 s, echo time = 30 ms, field of view = 220 mm × 220 mm, flip angle = 77°, matrix size = 64 × 64, slice thickness = 4 mm, slice gap = 1 mm, with a total of 30 slices. For anatomical imaging, a T1-weighted gradient echo sequence was used. The acquisition direction of the functional images was consistent with that of the anatomical images, with the following parameters: repetition time = 2.1 s, echo time = 4.6 ms, matrix size = 256 × 256, field of view = 230 mm × 230 mm, flip angle = 8°, slice thickness = 1 mm.

sec.title: fMRI Data Analysis Preprocessing
sec.p: Using the GRETNA toolbox (http://www.nitrc.org/projects/gretna/) on the MATLAB 2013b platform (The MathWorks, Inc., Natick, MA, USA), the initial resting-state fMRI data were converted from the “dicom” format to the “*.nii” format. To eliminate the effects of initial signal instability and the participants’ adaptation to the environment, the first 10 resting-state images were discarded. Subsequently, each image underwent slice timing correction, and the corrected data were realigned to correct for head motion. Data with head motion exceeding 1.5 degrees of rotation or 3 mm of translation were excluded. The data were then normalized, spatially standardized, smoothed, detrended to remove linear drift, and filtered using a band-pass filter for further preprocessing. Finally, time points with excessive head motion were removed through scrubbing to further minimize the impact of head motion.

sec.title: Functional Connectivity Matrix
sec.p: The GRETNA toolbox was used to perform correlation analysis on the time series between each pair of brain nodes. Common correlation coefficients include Pearson, Spearman, and Kendall. Since Spearman and Kendall correlation coefficients typically measure the monotonic relationship between two variables, this study used the Pearson correlation coefficient to measure the correlation between any two brain regions. Based on the anatomical automatic labeling template, the entire brain was abstracted into a brain network containing 90 nodes. The functional connections between the 90 brain regions were defined as the edges of the brain network. The blood oxygen level-dependent time series of all voxels within each node were extracted, and the pairwise correlations of the time series were used to construct the functional connectivity matrix.

sec.title: Risks of Acupuncture
sec.p: Acupuncture may cause adverse reactions such as mental fatigue, dizziness, pale complexion, decreased blood pressure, and excessive sweating or subcutaneous bleeding at the acupuncture site leading to swelling, pain, and bruising. Additionally, excessive needle manipulation by the practitioner may cause fainting. The operator will assess these situations and provide appropriate treatment and documentation.

sec.title: Safety Monitoring
sec.p: During the study, close attention was paid to the frequency and severity of adverse events experienced by patients. Any adverse events that occurred were meticulously recorded in the “Adverse Event Record Form” and addressed accordingly.

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: Data analysis was conducted using SPSS (IBM SPSS Statistics 26.0 for Windows) software by research data processors who were blinded to the group assignments, ensuring the implementation of the blinding method. Initially, summary statistics for all variables were cleaned and processed, checking for missing values and supplementing and perfecting the original data as necessary. The normality of the data was assessed by calculating the deviation of sample data from the theoretical values of a normal distribution, and the Shapiro-Wilk test was used to determine if the data followed a normal distribution. For data that met the normal distribution, the mean ± standard deviation (X ± S) was used for description; for data that did not meet the normal distribution, the Wilcoxon rank-sum test was used, and the median (M) and interquartile range (lower quartile P25, upper quartile P75) were used for description. For the comparison of count data, the chi-square test was used, and the sample size (n) was used for description. All p values calculated during the data analysis process were corrected for multiple comparisons. All statistical tests were two tailed, with a significance level α set at 0.05; a p value <0.05 was considered statistically significant. Analysis of covariance was employed, controlling for covariates such as gender, age, and education level, to accurately assess the independent effect of the intervention. Intergroup comparisons were conducted using an independent-samples t test. Multiple imputation will be utilized to handle missing data, thereby reducing potential bias and ensuring dataset completeness.

sec.title: Data Collection and Data Management
sec.p: In this study, data collection will be conducted by data evaluators using the Case Report Form tool. Subsequently, research assistants will enter these data into the data collection system provided by the Clinical Research Electronic Management Public Platform (ResMan) of the Chinese Clinical Trial Registration Center (URL: http://www.medresman.org.cn). After the study is completed, all data generated during this study will be publicly released through this platform for further analysis and validation.

sec.title: Dissemination
sec.p: The findings of this study will be published in journals and conference proceedings related to cognitive rehabilitation. The results will be shared with the public through various online and offline methods, including but not limited to academic conferences, online courses, video conferences, and health education sessions. The study protocol has been registered and can be accessed through the Chinese Clinical Trial Registration Center Website (registered at: ChiCTR.org.cn, Registration Number: ChiCTR2400092961).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: In our preliminary studies, resting-state fMRI and cognitive assessments have demonstrated that acupuncture can activate brain regions associated with cognition. The changes in brain networks following acupuncture are characterized by increased connectivity between multiple brain regions. The application of acupuncture can modulate brain networks by enhancing the connectivity between cognition-related brain regions, thereby improving cognitive impairments in patients [33]. Acupuncture has been found to have a positive effect on cognitive impairments in the elderly. However, research on acupuncture treatment for long COVID syndrome is currently in its preliminary stages. In this study, we designed an acupuncture intervention for patients with MCIs due to long COVID, aiming to investigate the role of acupuncture in alleviating cognitive impairments in patients with long COVID. Additionally, we employed fMRI technology to explore the mechanisms of this intervention from the perspectives of the default mode network and the dorsal attention networks associated with episodic memory in patients with MCIs.

During the trial, the blinding rule will be strictly followed. The specific grouping details of the trial will be concealed from the study evaluators and data analysts to reduce subjective bias and enhance the reliability and scientific validity of the research results. During the treatment process, the acupuncturist is a licensed traditional Chinese medicine practitioner with over 15 years of acupuncture experience. Patients receive acupuncture treatment three times a week on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays for a total of 8 weeks. Compared to pharmacological treatments, acupuncture has a lower likelihood of causing adverse reactions such as gastrointestinal disturbances and hepatic or renal dysfunction, making it a relatively safe treatment option. This is particularly beneficial for patients with long COVID, especially the elderly and those with chronic underlying conditions, as acupuncture is highly safe with minimal side effects. Moreover, the cost of acupuncture treatment is relatively low. When compared to expensive pharmacological treatments or high-tech medical equipment therapies, acupuncture offers a significant economic advantage. For patients with long COVID who require long-term treatment, this can greatly reduce their financial burden.

Therefore, this study will yield direct neuroimaging evidence demonstrating that acupuncture improves cognitive function via neuroplastic pathways. It is expected to identify key impaired brain networks associated with cognitive impairment in post-COVID-19 condition and further elucidate acupuncture’s modulatory effects on the functional activity and connectivity strength of these specific networks. The findings may reveal distinct brain network features correlated with the severity of cognitive deficits or treatment response, which could serve as potential objective biomarkers for future auxiliary diagnosis or outcome prediction. This research will provide outcomes with practical value for guiding clinical practice.

sec.title: Acknowledgments
sec.p: The authors sincerely thank the staff of the community service centers who participated in this study. We are grateful for the support provided by the clinical trial team at The Second Affiliated Hospital of Shenzhen University.

sec.title: Statement of Ethics
sec.p: This study protocol complies with the Declaration of Helsinki. It has been approved by the Medical Ethics Committee of The Second Affiliated Hospital of Shenzhen University (Approval No. BYL20230815). All participants will provide written consent after being fully informed (trial registration: Chinese Clinical Trial Registry (http://www.chictr.org.cn), Registration Number: ChiCTR2400092961, Date of Registration: 2024-11-26).

sec.title: Conflict of Interest Statement
sec.p: The authors have no conflicts of interest to declare.

sec.title: Funding Sources
sec.p: Funding sources were as follows: National Natural Science Foundation of China (81973922); Shenzhen Bao'an District Medical Association (BAYXH2023011); Shenzhen Bao’an District Traditional Chinese Medicine Development Foundation (2022KJCX-ZJZL-14); and Shenzhen Sanming Project (2022).

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: Shangjie Chen and Lei Xu designed the study protocol and conceived the research. Jing Chen wrote the manuscript and participated in the study’s implementation. Dan Wang assisted with the data processing section of the manuscript. Ziang Zhang and Mengru Shen handled participant recruitment. Yingqi Lu contributed to revising the study protocol. All authors participated in drafting the manuscript and read and approved the final version.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: The corresponding author will provide research datasets underlying this work to qualified investigators following reasonable validation of intended usage.
